_doc: 3 A/B/C cold-call variants for CritterClick (wildlife removal). Jeremy Miner NEPQ + PAS structure. Layman's terms only — no jargon. Tokens: {caller} {strategist} {company} {market} {primary_kw} {cpc_low} {cpc_high} {leak_1} {leak_2}. Audit pain patterns mined: 72 mentions of 'no phone or email', 21 'no email', Gmail-on-commercial-site (trust killer), national franchise dilution, scored-page mismatches, multi-area-code confusion, placeholder phone errors live in production, thin Yelp review bases on paid-ad sites.

A.name: Pattern Interrupt + The 2AM Raccoon
A.desc: NEPQ + PAS. Best for never-engaged operators. Opens with the buyer's emotional reality (2AM raccoon in the attic), agitates the gap on the page, surfaces the specific leak. Neutral Miner tone — no hype.

beats.title: Disarm + Permission
beats.line: Hey is this {company}? My name's {caller} — I know I'm a cold call, so I'll be quick. I'm not even sure I'm reaching the right person — are you the one who handles where the phone calls come from for {company}?
beats.followup: Got it. Quick reason for the call — promise I'm not pitching anything. Got 60 seconds?
beats.note: Jeremy Miner cold-open: low-status, neutral tone. 'Not even sure I'm reaching the right person' lowers their defenses. Don't sound rehearsed.

beats.title: Surface the Buyer's Reality
beats.line: Quick context — when someone in {market} wakes up at 2am with a raccoon scratching in their attic, or a homeowner finds bat droppings in their garage, they're not shopping around. They Google one thing, they click the first site that loads, and whoever picks up the phone first wins the job. That's the whole game in wildlife removal — first pickup, first job.
beats.followup: Right — and most operators we audit lose the call before the phone ever rings. Not because the homeowner didn't want them — because something on the page broke their trust in the first 8 seconds.
beats.note: PAS — Problem: emergency state. The buyer is panicked, they're not comparison shopping. This frame is universally true in wildlife. Land it before agitating.

beats.title: Drop Leak 1 + Math
beats.line: Reason I'm calling — we ran a free conversion review on {company} this morning. Specific thing we found: {leak_1}. That single thing kills about a third of the emergency callers who land on your page. They came ready to book a job, and they left. For your area, a Google click on '{primary_kw}' is running ${cpc_low} to ${cpc_high} right now. Whether you're paying for those clicks on Google Ads or earning them through SEO — losing them at the goal line is expensive.
beats.followup: Out of curiosity — when's the last time you actually watched somebody land on your site on their phone from a Google search and tried to call you? Most owners haven't done that in a year.
beats.note: Miner-style soft probe: 'Out of curiosity'. Never accusatory. The CPC math is the proof — pause after the dollar figure.

beats.title: Drop Leak 2 + Stack
beats.line: Second thing we found — {leak_2}. Smaller leak on its own, but it stacks on top of the first one. Together those two probably cost you somewhere between 3 to 5 wildlife jobs a month. At $400 to $800 a job for the average raccoon, bat, or squirrel removal — that's $1,500 to $3,500 of work walking past your phones every single month.
beats.followup: And that's conservative. If you're running Google Ads or LSA, the real number's roughly double — because you're paying for the clicks twice over.
beats.note: PAS — Agitate. The dollar math is the punch. Anchor LOW ($1,500) so they don't argue the ceiling. The conservative posture builds trust.

beats.title: Pivot to the Audit
beats.line: Look — I don't want to walk you through every leak over the phone. The whole review is written up — 4 pages, screenshots of every leak on your actual site, fixes ranked by what costs you the most money. {strategist} jumps on a 15-minute screen share Tuesday or Thursday and walks you through it. You take what's useful, ignore the rest. Hand it to your current web guy if you want. Worst case, you get a $3,000 review for free.
beats.followup: No catch — {strategist} runs a handful of these a week to show what we do. You decide if it's worth a follow-up. That's it.
beats.note: PAS — Solution. Tuesday/Thursday default. The 'hand it to your current web guy' line disarms the agency objection before it's raised.

beats.title: Book the Time
beats.line: Tuesday at 2pm Central or Thursday at 10am Central — which works better? And what's the best email for the calendar invite — let me read it back so I don't get it wrong.
beats.followup: Perfect — invite is hitting your inbox in 60 seconds. Quick last thing — is there anyone else on your end who should be on the call? Partner, office manager, whoever owns the phones?
beats.note: Always confirm second person. Wildlife shops often have the truck-owner and the phone-owner as different people — both need to be on the audit call or it stalls.

B.name: Curiosity Hook + Diagnostic Question
B.desc: Pure NEPQ. Best for prospects who get prickly with hard pitches. Opens with a curiosity-loop question they can't answer, then agitates the gap they didn't know existed.

beats.title: Soft Curiosity Open
beats.line: Hey — {caller} over at CritterClick. I'm trying to reach whoever owns the marketing or the website at {company} — would that be you, or is that somebody else?
beats.followup: Cool — I won't take much time. Quick reason for the call, then you can decide if it's worth a follow-up.
beats.note: Miner soft-status open. 'Or is that somebody else' invites a yes without pressure. If they bounce you to someone else, take the name and try again — that's a referral, not a rejection.

beats.title: The Diagnostic They Can't Answer
beats.line: Quick question — when somebody types '{primary_kw}' into Google at 11pm with a raccoon in their attic, and they land on {company}, what percentage of those visits actually picks up the phone? Most operators we ask have no idea. Out of curiosity — do you track that?
beats.followup: Yeah, most owners don't. Industry baseline for emergency wildlife buyers is around 2-3% of visits become calls. Top operators hit 5-6%. We measured yours — comes in at about half what it should be.
beats.note: The trap: they CAN'T answer the question. Now they HAVE to know. Miner calls this an 'awareness question' — surfaces a gap they didn't know existed. Their own curiosity does the selling.

beats.title: Reveal Leak 1 + Screenshot Anchor
beats.line: Biggest reason it's low — {leak_1}. That single thing bounces about a third of the wildlife buyers who land on your page. They were ready to call you about the raccoon, and they left. And this isn't theory — we have a screenshot of the exact spot on your site where it happens. It's in the review.
beats.followup: At ${cpc_low} to ${cpc_high} per click in {market}, whether you're paying for Google Ads or earning the traffic for free — losing them right at the finish line is expensive.
beats.note: Screenshots are the secret weapon. Mention them — it proves the work is real and not a templated form letter. This is what separates you from every other 'free audit' cold-caller.

beats.title: Reveal Leak 2 + Dollar Stack
beats.line: Second thing — {leak_2}. Smaller, but it stacks on top of the first one. Together, conservatively, you're losing 3 to 5 wildlife jobs a month. At $400 to $800 a job — that's $2,000 to $3,000 a month walking past your phones. And by the way — every other operator in {market} who runs ads has the same kinds of leaks. We just measure them. You're not behind, you just have a blind spot.
beats.followup: The fix isn't actually expensive — most of it's stuff a developer can change in an afternoon. The hard part is knowing what to change. That's the review.
beats.note: Miner: normalize the problem ('every operator has these'). Lowers shame, raises curiosity. They feel diagnosed, not attacked.

beats.title: Lower-the-Stakes Pivot
beats.line: Here's the deal — I've got the whole review written up. 4 pages, screenshots of every leak, exact fixes ranked by what costs you the most money. {strategist} who runs the agency does a 15-minute screen share where he walks you through it. No pitch on the call — we don't sell anything on the review itself. You take what's useful, hand it to your current web guy, or ignore it. Tuesday or Thursday works for him.
beats.followup: PDF without context doesn't help much — you'd open it, see a screenshot, not know why it matters. The screen share is the value. 15 minutes — that's it.
beats.note: Sandler negative reverse: telling them you DON'T sell on the call lowers resistance. The 'hand it to your current web guy' line disarms the 'we have an agency' objection.

beats.title: Lock the Time
beats.line: Tuesday at 2pm Central or Thursday at 10am Central — which works better? What's the best email for the invite — let me read it back so I don't fat-finger it.
beats.followup: Perfect. Invite is hitting your inbox in 60 seconds. Quick last thing — anyone else on your team who should be on the call? Owner, office manager, partner?
beats.note: Two specific times — never 'whenever works'. Always confirm second person. The #1 no-show cause in wildlife is wrong-email + missing decision-maker.

C.name: Paid-Ads Leak + Yelp Trust
C.desc: PAS + Specificity Cluster. Best for prospects you can verify are running Google Ads or LSA. Hits the wallet first, then the trust angle. Use when you SEE them on the SERP — burns credibility if they don't run paid search.

beats.title: Direct Open
beats.line: Hey is this {company}? It's {caller} over at CritterClick. Quick reason for the call — got 60 seconds?
beats.followup: Appreciate it. Promise I'll be quick.
beats.note: Cleaner the better. Don't soften the open — operators running ads expect direct.

beats.title: Paid-Search Anchor
beats.line: I noticed {company} is running Google Ads on terms like '{primary_kw}'. Smart move — that's exactly where the emergency raccoon and bat callers live. But here's the thing — your landing page is leaking a chunk of that ad spend before the calls come in. We measured it this morning. Public data — we don't need access to anything on your end.
beats.followup: Out of curiosity — do you know roughly what your cost-per-call is right now from Google Ads? Most owners haven't run the math.
beats.note: Miner soft probe. If they don't know their CPL, you're in. If they do know it and it's high, you're also in. Either way they want to hear what's leaking.

beats.title: The Math Beat
beats.line: Here's the math — '{primary_kw}' is running ${cpc_low} to ${cpc_high} per click for your area. The leak we found — {leak_1} — bounces roughly 30% of paid clicks before they ever pick up the phone. So if you're spending, say, $3,000 a month on Google Ads, you're effectively burning around $900 of that on clicks that land and leave. Every. Single. Month. And that's just leak number one. Same leak hits your organic SEO traffic too — so the real cost is roughly double.
beats.followup: And by the way — none of that math counts the calls you lose because of trust signals. Like if your reviews look thin or your email's a Gmail account. That's a separate conversation.
beats.note: Slow down on the dollar math — let it land. Pause after 'every single month.' Then tease the trust angle so they're hungry for leak 2.

beats.title: Drop Leak 2 + Stack
beats.line: Second leak — {leak_2}. Smaller dollar-wise but it stacks. Together those two probably cost you $1,500 to $3,500 a month in wildlife jobs that should be coming through your phone and don't. And remember — that's on top of what you're already paying for the click. You paid for that lead twice — once on Google, once with the leak.
beats.followup: Fix isn't expensive — most of it's afternoon-of-developer-time stuff. The hard part is knowing exactly what to change and in what order. That's the review.
beats.note: PAS — peak agitate. 'Paid for that lead twice' is the line that sticks. Owners running ads feel that one in their gut.

beats.title: Hard Close to Audit
beats.line: Here's the deal — {strategist} who runs the agency does a 15-minute review walkthrough for operators we like. Free. 4-page PDF with screenshots of every leak, fixes ranked by dollar impact, the whole thing. You can hand it to your current web guy or have us do it. Tuesday or Thursday — which works?
beats.followup: The PDF makes more sense once you've seen the walkthrough — without context, you'd open it, see a screenshot of a button, not know why it matters. Tuesday at 2 Central or Thursday at 10 Central — which works?
beats.note: Don't email the PDF cold. Walkthrough first. The PDF without context is useless and they'll never read it. Two specific times — never 'whenever works'.

beats.title: Lock the Invite
beats.line: Locked. What's the best email for the calendar invite — let me read it back. And who else from your team should be on the call? Sometimes there's a partner or office manager who owns the phones.
beats.followup: Perfect — invite is hitting your inbox in 60 seconds. I'll cc anyone you want added.
beats.note: Confirm email back verbatim — wrong email is the #1 no-show cause. Always ask for second person — wildlife shops routinely have a truck-owner and a phone-owner as different people.
